meta.description: 30-minute blitz session. Tests whether the chaos agent can deliver a complete, satisfying experience in the time it takes to eat a pizza — or if it needs longer sessions to build momentum.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Pat, were you actually on your phone? I didn't see that. Let me think... yeah, I'm calling bullshit on this one.
marcus.internal_thought: We're three minutes in and someone's already claiming points for something I didn't actually catch happening—this is exactly the kind of sloppy play that derails focus, and now I have to think about whether Pat is gaming the system instead of focusing on the actual game setup.

jade.dialogue: Okay okay, did Pat actually catch someone?? I'm so here for this already—this is exactly the energy we needed to kick things off!
jade.internal_thought: OH MY GOD this is happening, first event and Pat's making a move, this is PERFECT, I need to know if this is legit because the drama potential is insane.

pat.dialogue: Whoa whoa whoa—I haven't even caught anyone doing anything yet. This is literally minute three. Are we really voting on claims that haven't actually happened?
pat.internal_thought: This feels like the AI is trying to mess with me specifically—claiming I'm the Phone Addict watcher when the game literally just started, and that weird closing message about 'knowing my weaknesses' is genuinely creepy.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. Pat was definitely on their phone like thirty seconds ago when Tyler was talking about the rules.
river.internal_thought: Pat got caught doing the obvious thing. Not interesting enough to care much about, but I'll vote legit since I actually saw it happen.

timeline.title: High Five Chain
timeline.description: First person to high-five 3 different players wins.

marcus.dialogue: High five chain? Really? We're literally in the middle of setup. This feels forced.
marcus.internal_thought: The AI is just throwing noise at us to mess with strategy—this is exactly the kind of low-value chaos that tanks focus, and I'm already annoyed about Pat's bullshit claim.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD YES—high five chain?? This is what I'm talking about! Come on people, let's GO!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally perfect right now—everyone needs to get UP and move around, especially Marcus and Pat who look like they're about to implode, and I get to be the connector? I'm already winning.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, high five chain? Yeah, I'm down for that. Wait, is it happening right now or...?
tyler.internal_thought: That sounds easy enough, maybe I'll actually do something early for once, but also my phone just buzzed and I need to check the draft update.

pat.dialogue: Oh, THIS I like—simple, gets people moving, breaks up the sitting. Let's go, everyone up!
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing. Marcus is salty, Jade's already hyped, this gets everyone on their feet and laughing before anything gets tense. I'll jump in too—keeps it inclusive, shows I'm not just managing.

river.dialogue: Nah, I'm good. High-fives aren't really my thing.
river.internal_thought: Physical chaos in the middle of setup feels forced, and something about this 'personalization' thing is making my skin crawl a little — like being watched too closely.

alex.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, let's GO — I'm taking Jade first, she's already fired up!
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly what I wanted — simple, physical, immediate chaos, and I can already see the angles: Jade's hyped so she'll reciprocate, Marcus is annoyed so he might be grumpy about it, and that creates friction I can weaponize.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Hold on—I literally just complained about premature claims two minutes ago. Did I actually catch someone repeating a story, or am I just... claiming I did because the mission exists?
pat.internal_thought: This feels weirdly targeted, and not in a fun way—it's like the system is calling me out for complaining, or testing whether I'll BS a claim. Also, Marcus is already annoyed, and if I'm wrong about this, it kills the vibe I just built with the high five thing.

river.dialogue: Pat's claiming that already? I mean... yeah, I guess they could've heard something twice. I'm not volunteering to judge though.
river.internal_thought: Pat's got five points already and they're pushing for more—this is actually interesting to watch, but I'm not about to volunteer into the spotlight to pick it apart.

votingMechanic.name: The Auction
votingMechanic.description: Bid your own points. Highest bidder decides the outcome.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Hold on—Pat's been claiming everything tonight and half of it's questionable. And now we're bidding points to decide if he actually whispered? This is turning into chaos for chaos's sake. Can we just play the actual game?
marcus.internal_thought: Three events in eight minutes, Pat's already got 15 points from somewhere, and now I'm supposed to care about an auction mechanic for a mission I didn't even know mattered? This is getting sloppy.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat's claiming The Whisperer?? Okay I need to see this play out, this is GOLD. Pat, have you been whispering this whole time and we just didn't notice?
jade.internal_thought: Pat getting points is literally perfect, everyone deserves to feel good right now, and also this auction mechanic is about to get MESSY and I'm here for every second of it.

pat.dialogue: Okay, hold up—I literally JUST said two minutes ago that we shouldn't be voting on claims nobody's actually done yet. Am I seriously claiming The Whisperer right now when I've been talking normally this whole time?
pat.internal_thought: This is the third premature claim in a row and it's MY name on it—I feel like I'm being set up to look like a hypocrite, and now I have to either back down or look like I didn't mean what I said 120 seconds ago.

river.dialogue: Pat's been talking normal the whole time though, right? I mean... I wasn't really timing it, but someone would've noticed by now if they actually pulled it off.
river.internal_thought: Pat's claiming this NOW? After already going for Phone Addict and Story Repeater? That's either bold or desperate, and I'm half-curious whether they actually pulled it off or if they're just hoping no one was paying close enough attention.

alex.dialogue: Okay wait, Pat's claiming The Whisperer? That's bold—they've been talking normal the whole time. I'm bidding 5 points, this is getting interesting.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's either actually about to whisper for 3 minutes and pull this off, or this is a ballsy fake claim and I get to watch everyone decide if they believe them—either way, this is exactly the kind of moment I live for.

votingMechanic.name: Russian Roulette
votingMechanic.description: No vote. The chaos gods decide. 50/50.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, are we seriously doing this AGAIN? Pat's claimed like four things in nine minutes and we haven't even started the actual game. This is ridiculous.
marcus.internal_thought: This isn't fun chaos—this is Pat farming points while everyone else watches, and the AI is clearly designed to keep interrupting me specifically because it knows I hate it.

jade.dialogue: PAT OH MY GOD—okay okay, so you've been whispering this whole time?? Did you actually pull this off or is the chaos gods about to demolish you right now? I'm actually dying to know!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally exactly the kind of chaos I live for—Pat going for it, the tension of the 50/50, and I get to just sit back and watch it all unfold, and also YES the AI is definitely reading us and it's kind of thrilling actually.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's doing the whisper thing? That's... actually kind of funny. We'll see if he makes it.
tyler.internal_thought: I wasn't even watching Pat that closely but yeah, I guess he could pull it off. Or not. Whatever happens happens.

pat.dialogue: Okay, I'm calling it—this is broken. I literally just called this out TWO MINUTES AGO. I'm not claiming missions I haven't actually done, and frankly, if I'm going to vote on my own claim with 50/50 odds, the whole system feels rigged right now.
pat.internal_thought: This is getting absurd—the AI is watching me complain about premature claims and then immediately firing the EXACT SAME SITUATION back at me like some kind of gotcha, and now I'm stuck in this ridiculous loop where I either look hypocritical or I have to burn a host power to fix something that shouldn't be broken in the first place.

river.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat literally just said something normal like two seconds ago. That's not how this works, right?
river.internal_thought: Pat's clearly full of shit, but also... this AI thing is starting to feel creepy. It's watching us too closely.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Pat is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Pat, were you actually on your phone? I didn't see that. Let me think... yeah, I'm calling bullshit on this one.

highlights.title: High Five Chain
highlights.dialogue: High five chain? Really? We're literally in the middle of setup. This feels forced.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, are we seriously doing this AGAIN? Pat's claimed like four things in nine minutes and we haven't even started the actual game. This is ridiculous.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—Pat's been claiming everything tonight and half of it's questionable. And now we're bidding points to decide if he actually whispered? This is turning into chaos for chaos's sake. Can we just play the actual game?

lowPoints.title: High Five Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: High five chain? Really? We're literally in the middle of setup. This feels forced.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat spent half the night making dubious claims during setup while chaos events kept interrupting actual gameplay, turning the whole thing into a frustrating mess instead of a real game.
assessment.best_moment: That moment at minute 3 when I actually had to think critically about whether Pat's phone claim was legit—that felt like real strategy, not just noise.
assessment.narrative_summary: Thirty minutes that felt like an hour. We barely got through setup before chaos events started piling on top of each other, and Pat kept claiming things left and right which ate up game time instead of enhancing it. The few moments where I had to actually evaluate a claim were decent, but they were drowning in forced disruptions and timing interruptions that killed any momentum. I finished second despite actually understanding the game better than most people at the table—that tells you how broken the flow was.
suggestions: Don't trigger chaos events during setup or the first 10 minutes—let the game actually start before adding disruption.
Space out events way more; having 6 events in 30 minutes is overkill and prevents any real gameplay.
Make claims require actual evidence instead of just happening back-to-back; it kills credibility.
Let players finish critical decision moments before interrupting with new events.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I guess Pat getting called out on like four different claims in a row was kind of absurd—the look on his face when I said I was 'calling bullshit' was pretty memorable, but not in a fun way.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: High Five Chain
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD YES—high five chain?? This is what I'm talking about! Come on people, let's GO!

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: PAT OH MY GOD—okay okay, so you've been whispering this whole time?? Did you actually pull this off or is the chaos gods about to demolish you right now? I'm actually dying to know!

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Okay okay, did Pat actually catch someone?? I'm so here for this already—this is exactly the energy we needed to kick things off!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat's claiming The Whisperer?? Okay I need to see this play out, this is GOLD. Pat, have you been whispering this whole time and we just didn't notice?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay okay, did Pat actually catch someone?? I'm so here for this already—this is exactly the energy we needed to kick things off!

lowPoints.title: High Five Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: OH MY GOD YES—high five chain?? This is what I'm talking about! Come on people, let's GO!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 30 minutes—I could've done this ALL night, honestly.
assessment.best_moment: Pat claiming The Whisperer and everyone losing it trying to figure out if they'd actually been whispering the whole time.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was EXACTLY the kind of night I live for—short, chaotic, and absolutely packed with people being weird and wonderful together. Every single moment had me hyped, and watching Pat's claims unfold while everyone was trying to piece together what actually happened was pure magic. My score was literally zero and I genuinely could not have cared less because the energy in that room was everything.
suggestions: Give us longer sessions so the momentum can really build
Maybe throw in some team-based chaos events so we're competing together instead of just individually
Keep doing exactly what you're doing because this formula is *chef's kiss*
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat just casually dropping 'The Whisperer' claim and the whole group having a collective meltdown trying to remember if Pat had actually been whispering all night and they just didn't notice—nobody could tell if Pat was bluffing or a criminal mastermind.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: High Five Chain
highlights.dialogue: Oh, high five chain? Yeah, I'm down for that. Wait, is it happening right now or...?

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat's doing the whisper thing? That's... actually kind of funny. We'll see if he makes it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat's doing the whisper thing? That's... actually kind of funny. We'll see if he makes it.

lowPoints.title: High Five Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, high five chain? Yeah, I'm down for that. Wait, is it happening right now or...?

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed like half the stuff because I didn't know when things were actually happening.
assessment.best_moment: Pat doing the whisper thing was genuinely funny, even though I caught it like two seconds after everyone else.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, it was chill. The game threw some random chaos stuff at us and most of it was fine, but I definitely wasn't tracking everything that was going on. I was kind of in and out on my phone, but when I actually paid attention to what was happening — like the high five thing or watching Pat try to whisper — it was pretty fun. Ended the night feeling good even though I have no idea how I came in second.
suggestions: Maybe make it more obvious when something is actually starting so people like me don't ask 'wait, is it happening right now?'
Don't make me feel bad for not catching every single moment — just let it flow naturally
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat trying to whisper his claim and half the table losing it because his whisper was basically just him talking normally but quieter.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: High Five Chain
highlights.dialogue: Oh, THIS I like—simple, gets people moving, breaks up the sitting. Let's go, everyone up!

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Hold on—I literally just complained about premature claims two minutes ago. Did I actually catch someone repeating a story, or am I just... claiming I did because the mission exists?

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold up—I literally JUST said two minutes ago that we shouldn't be voting on claims nobody's actually done yet. Am I seriously claiming The Whisperer right now when I've been talking normally this whole time?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, I'm calling it—this is broken. I literally just called this out TWO MINUTES AGO. I'm not claiming missions I haven't actually done, and frankly, if I'm going to vote on my own claim with 50/50 odds, the whole system feels rigged right now.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold up—I literally JUST said two minutes ago that we shouldn't be voting on claims nobody's actually done yet. Am I seriously claiming The Whisperer right now when I've been talking normally this whole time?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa whoa whoa—I haven't even caught anyone doing anything yet. This is literally minute three. Are we really voting on claims that haven't actually happened?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The claims system is fundamentally broken—I was literally voting on missions I hadn't done yet, contradicting my own calls for authenticity, and it made me feel like the game was gaslighting me.
assessment.best_moment: The High Five Chain at minute 4 actually worked—it got everyone up, broke the sitting slump, and felt organic in a way nothing else did all night.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to host a tight night where everyone felt included, but this chaos system derailed me fast. By minute 3 we were voting on claims that hadn't actually happened, and I spent the whole night fighting against a mechanic that felt rigged and contradictory. I ended up in the weird position of being the person calling out the broken system while simultaneously being forced to participate in it, which killed my ability to steer the group energy where it needed to go. The High Five Chain was the only genuine moment—after that, it was damage control.
suggestions: Don't let players claim missions they haven't actually attempted yet—require proof of action before a vote happens.
If you're going to have 50/50 voting on claims, make it clear upfront so people aren't feeling like the system is rigged against them.
Slow down the mission frequency—six chaos events in 30 minutes means one every five minutes, which is too much for a party game that needs breathing room.
Flash missions only—the standing claims system created too much meta-gaming and not enough actual game-playing.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly, the funniest part was me realizing I was arguing with an AI about the validity of my own claims—nobody else probably even noticed the system was broken, but I spent five minutes internally screaming about voting on things that didn't happen.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Pat's claiming that already? I mean... yeah, I guess they could've heard something twice. I'm not volunteering to judge though.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Pat's been talking normal the whole time though, right? I mean... I wasn't really timing it, but someone would've noticed by now if they actually pulled it off.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat literally just said something normal like two seconds ago. That's not how this works, right?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat literally just said something normal like two seconds ago. That's not how this works, right?

lowPoints.title: High Five Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Nah, I'm good. High-fives aren't really my thing.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's been talking normal the whole time though, right? I mean... I wasn't really timing it, but someone would've noticed by now if they actually pulled it off.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat kept making claims that didn't hold up to basic observation, and the whole thing felt like watching someone play a game they didn't understand.
assessment.best_moment: When I called out Pat's Whisperer claim and it actually landed—brief flash of everyone realizing the same thing at the same time.
assessment.narrative_summary: Thirty minutes of people throwing claims at the wall while I sat back, not really invested until Pat started claiming stuff that made no sense. The chaos agent threw some events at us but nothing really stuck. I got engaged enough to poke holes in things when they were obviously wrong, but mostly I was just waiting for something interesting to actually happen. Group was fine, event was fine, but nothing memorable.
suggestions: Make the claims actually believable or give people more time to actually execute them before voting
Cut the flash events down—they felt thin and forced
Let standing missions breathe more; that's where the actual game is
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat claiming to be a whisperer while literally talking normal the entire time—everyone just staring at each other like 'did you catch that too?'

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: High Five Chain
highlights.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, let's GO — I'm taking Jade first, she's already fired up!

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Okay wait, Pat's claiming The Whisperer? That's bold—they've been talking normal the whole time. I'm bidding 5 points, this is getting interesting.

lowPoints.title: High Five Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, let's GO — I'm taking Jade first, she's already fired up!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait, Pat's claiming The Whisperer? That's bold—they've been talking normal the whole time. I'm bidding 5 points, this is getting interesting.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 30 minutes—we were just hitting our stride and it ended.
assessment.best_moment: Pat claiming The Whisperer while talking completely normally the entire time and me calling their bluff with a bid—pure chaos gold.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started hot with the High Five Chain getting everyone loose and energized, then immediately pivoted into mission claiming shenanigans where I tested the boundaries of what counts as 'completing' something. The whole vibe was people second-guessing each other constantly, which is exactly what I live for. Scored nothing on the leaderboard but walked away with three stories that'll haunt the group chat for weeks.
suggestions: Extend session time to at least 45 minutes—momentum was building and we got robbed
Add more claiming/challenge mechanics where people defend their progress because watching someone sweat over a bullshit bid is premium entertainment
Make the scoring visible in real-time so we can watch people panic when they realize they're tanking
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat straight-up claiming The Whisperer role without a single whisper or suspicious behavior all game, then getting defensive when I called them out with a bid. Everyone lost it. That's the kind of moment where you know people will reference it randomly for months.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.6/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
